feedback.text: I believe that this is an important initiative that should be applied especially to video games as the gaming industry is reportedly worth more than the movie and music industry combined. However, protections against unfair practices are insufficient and this initiative would help. I will try to give multiple examples of such cases here: Street Fighter 6 is a fighting game that contains the option to get Fighter Coins which are only available by paying with real money. With those coins, it is possible to get emotes, skins for characters, battle pass with limited time access, etc... Some skins with all colors for example cost 300 Fighter Coins. However, it is not possible to buy 300 Fighter Coins directly. The Playstation Store for example offers at minimum 250 coins for 4.99 euros and the next option is 610 coins for 11.99 euros. It is unfair to consumers in multiple ways: - 1st, the user has to either pay twice 4.99 to be able to pay the skin and have 500 coins or 11.99 for 610 coins - 2nd, if the user buys twice the 4.99 pack, they will have 500 coins and only be able to buy one skin. They will have 200 coins leftover, pressuring the consumer to pay more to not waste the 200 unused coins on the account. As a solution for example, the publisher could be forced to propose an option to the player that allows them to pay directly for the skins, as an equivalent to the value for the price in coins, in this case around 6 euros. Genshin Impact is considered a gacha game which contains a loot box mechanism that allows users to use Primogems to get a random item or character and it is gambling. You can buy Genesis Crystal with real money and they can be converted one to one in Primogems. However, there is no age verification for paying crystals meaning that children can spend money in this. Pegi for example gives the game a minimum age of 12 years old to play the game and mentions the presence of the previously mentioned system but it is not enough as with this current implementation, children can participate in the gambling system. Therefore, 18+ would be more fitting. Spots to spend currency to get specific characters are also available for a limited amount of time. The loot boxes are frequent in the game industry and another example is in Counter Strike 2, a free game which replaced 2012's Counter Strike Global Offensive. Both games have fundamentally the same mechanics. The player can open a weapon case using a key for that specific case; both are buyable using steam credits. It is possible to use real money to add funds to the steam credits and those steam credits can be used to buy digital games but also physical items like a steam deck for example. Once a weapon case is opened, a slot screen appears that randomly chooses a weapon skin that you can use in-game or sell on the steam market for steam credit. Again, there are no verification of the age of the user and steam credit can be compared to chips in a casino and spending credit in a case and a key would be like roulette. Those skins can be sold for hundred of euros in steam credit and children can buy possessions with it. Videos by the youtuber Coffeezilla reveal that underage gambling is a strong issue in counter strike (both games) and that casinos also have formed around gambling those skins on third party websites. The game doesn't display chances of drops in Europe, which is also unfair. Last point I would like to mention is mobile gaming ads. There have been multiple reports of ads that uses shock value of content that is unavailable in the game to get the user to click on the ad and this is probably something that should get looked into. I believe that those cases are fitting the initiative and are worth taking into account. I also believe that age verification should be implemented for content involving money but general access to internet should instead be moderated and restricted by who's in charge of the child using a parental control tool for example.
